commentBody: I don't know about everyone else, but this was rough for a Wednesday. I found myself going back and forth in 3 of the 4 corners. Nice little midweek challenge.

Is “POSER” a question? (In COMPOSER)

I’m not sure.

Cute idea, but I’m iffy on POSER as a noun for a question. I get it, but maybe it’s a stretch.
What happened to the Midi puzzle?
COMPROMISE. COM promise. Halfway happy.

Nice Wednesday offering.
Way to COMPRESS those themers!
Thanks, Joseph.

Isaac wrote:

“17A. [Trait for a good waiter?] does not refer to someone employed in a restaurant, but rather to someone who is good at waiting around. The answer here is PATIENCE.”


I think that even if the clue was intended to refer to people who are simply waiting around, it works fine for restaurant waiters as well, since customers can be very trying these days.

Loved this theme! And now…

COMBUST: That company really flamed out.

COMPROMISE: “Our customers may rest assured that their needs matter some fraction of ours.”

COMPOSER: “Where shall we look to find the most irritating hold music.”

COMPRESS: “Don’t let the public know that the CEO has to lie down with a cool cloth every afternoon.”

COMPARABLE: One day, the tortoise and the hare decided to run a race to see which one had the better internet service.

COMPOST: “Employees are hereby instructed to cease collecting coffee grounds and orange peels to take home.”
Perfect Wednesday puzzle.
I really loved the theme, and it made the puzzle a breeze to complete, with all the COMs making for easy fill.  A perfect example of how to make a relatively easy puzzle enjoyable.
I had trouble in northeast with sparge and its crossings. I thought theme was fun.

That was a fun puzzle.  Really cool theme.  Loved the aha moments of figuring out they were all DOTCOM + (Real answer to question) but with the COM also real words.  Well done.

A couple really tough areas.  Like having SPARGE cross with SOPOR.  SPARGE I've never heard of and SOPOR I've only heard of in like vocabulary quizzes for longer versions of the word so that was really hard to come up with.

Also I originally had RAP AT instead of TAP AT which made it really tough to finally get TOURNAMENTS.

And final square I got was because I had COMPOSES instead of COMPOSER and didn't know California resorts enough to realize DELMAS was wrong.

Am I crazy or did we see I DO CARE with the exact same clue in the exact same spot less than a week ago?!

Certainly not soporific. An excellent Wednesday puzzle. 

Thanks

Uh oh.

I actually thought I was working on a Friday.  At first I thought it was a little too easy for a Friday, then I thought it was about right.

Doesn't give me a lot of confidence going into the gauntlet of Thursday to Sunday.

Plus I'm trying to do puzzles from 2003.  Very humbling.  Humiliating, even.

I'll never brag about being able to do Friday/Saturday puzzles again.

I think my post got eaten by emus. Trying again--

Let's do more!

COMMODE: Standard company B$
COMMUTE: Stop spouting said company B$
COMPLAIN: Simplify
COMBINATION: At our company we consider all fellow citizens to eschew hetero- and homosexuality in favor of just a single label that covers both 

(Ok the last one may be a stretch but I couldn't resist)

Andrzej, how much do you weigh? I'm thinking of tossing Poles in the next Highlands tournament and hoping you'll be there.  Also, how tall are you? Just in case there's Pole jumping there too. 

Sparge, eh? And cabers. Love it. My new two favorite words. A puzzle with a lot of energy and a rich cache of words. Well done.

Very unusual that the only two instances of I DO CARE in the modern era are three days apart with very similar clues. Those who say I do care too often perhaps doth protest too much. What they are really saying, a la a certain fashion-savvy jacket-wearer (my wife calls her a "fashist") is "I don't care do u?"
Loved it!

Like a Tuesday with a theme?  One of my faster solve times. NW corner a little tricky. Needed the column for SPARGE.
I'm starting to see some of the common clue/answer combos and patterns.
Nice feeling.
Ahem - Elevators do not have cars, or cabs, they are moving staircases. Lifts on the other hand… :) Great puzzle thanks.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Maybe they are listening?

What a great throwback Wednesday!

Fun Thursday-lite gimmick.

Good misdirects.

SPARGE??

Frabjous day!!
What a fun puzzle!  Still laughing at re-parsing the theme words.  Loved having a few longer words that we just don't get often, and yes SPARGE was new to me too!

Sunday 4/26/2026: 
“It’s truly important to me” = I DO CARE

Wednesday 4/29/2026:
“Actually, this matters to me a lot” = I DO CARE

I haven’t jumped on to the “too easy” bandwagon because I get the reasons for appealing to a wider subscriber base. But if there is an anti-AI bandwagon, consider me on board.

I know there are programs that help with fill, and I understand how they can be helpful, even necessary, sometimes. But when clone entries appear in such close proximity, the puzzles start to look more like robotic churn and less like individual works of art. Editing should avoid duplicates like these, and I don’t mean by slightly tweaking the clue. This has happened numerous times lately, and it’s disappointing. Because believe me, I DO CARE.

Seeing a CABER toss in person is something you don't tend to forget, so that answer went right in. And it didn't hurt to have the same clue from the last appearance of OP-ED. SPARGE I might have heard (brewery tours?) but it and METONYM took a few crosses to come up with. I totally missed the revealer as I finished with the acrosses.

Nice Wednesday, Joseph, and thanks!

This was very well executed. It has a pitfall, which is that once you get the theme (which was obvious from the first entry) it has the potential to be too easy because not only can you fill in the COM automatically but it should be a big hint to all the theme fill. But, surprisingly, none of the theme answers were slam dunks for me despite this, and the constructor/editors wisely compensated by making the fill and clues around it more challenging than the usual Wednesday (SPARGE, METONYM and CABERS, among others). I also briefly had an image of Andrzej getting thrown by some Scotsmen on that last one, but I can actually thank some “Monkey Island” video game or another for remembering that one.
You know you’ve been doing this for too long when you get ELOPERS on that clue with no crosses; and I almost got the “seed” clue the same way but I had INABRACKET before TOURNAMENT—close but no cigar. Also had feints before LUNGES and happy to see epee as a clue not an entry.
Anyway a very entertaining Wednesday, thanks!
Mr. Gangi, I COMmend you!

TIL METONYM. I will probably forget it by Friday. And still trying to wrap my head around that a kitten heel is considered a form of STILETTO heel, just lower. 👠

Re the Midi
When you finally find it, make sure Overlays are turned on.

Re the Mini
Boo Hiss, Joel, for the 1 Across clue.

Very creative theme. Well executed. 
Seemed Wednesday enough for me. 

“They usually get no reception”.  Outstanding clue. 
Does lewis still do that clues off the week thing?  I hope this makes it.  Or maybe it’s just me.

ONO. it’s been a minute. Don’t remember seeing HUA. But it’s something I will forget in 10 minutes. 

Thank you Joseph. 
Doing this puzzle was a great 
*internet company seeking shelter
COMFORT. 
not
*internet company that manufactures baseball equipment
COMBAT.

Metonymy, metonymy,
Please use it to refer to me,
So indirect, allusively,
Polite and subtle. Please feel free
To show refinement when you see--
Not just a simple simile
Or even a synecdoche
But hear my plea. Metonymy!

(But diphthongs remain my first love.)

Kitten heels are low heels, not high heels.  They may be technically stillettos because they are small, narrow heels.  But usually what are refered to as  stilletto heels are several inches longer than kitten heels.

The optometrist set up his own URL in the link of an eye.

(It's a good web sight.)

While catching up on the accumulated weekend work of the commentariat, I came across a late-posted delight: Whoa Nellie's transformation of Monday's grid contents into 'King Lear'. Go here to share her amazing accomplishment: https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4fmnjd?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share Ms Nellie, I applaud you. This was wonderful.

I learned three new words today! Sparge, metonym, and sopor. Here they are in a sentence: “As Libby woke from a sopor, she wondered if her deep sleep was the result of too much or too little sparging in last night’s brew or if she were just a mere mortal destined to use ‘metonym’ in a sentence for once in her life.”
Always thought of triple jump as a field event not a track one.

As a fella who up until recently had a side gig as a tour guide at the 3rd largest craft brewery in MN, unlike most commenters here today, sparge was an easy one for me. :-)  I had never heard of caber though. Fun puzzle, and even with knowing the “com” start to the theme material, some of them were a little tough!
What a great puzzle. Loved it! Many thanks Mr Gangi.
New word for the day sparge.  The blog’s sp ck tried to change it to sparse 😜

TOURNAMENT and ELOPERS - so clever. Enjoyable crossword, with me getting help for a few, like NEMESIS (didn't watch Road Runner) and DELMAR. 

I found getting one theme clue a bit tricky - COMPRESS, also hadn't heard of PHOTODUMP (never used Instagram) or SOPOR although I guessed from soporific.

As a Scot, CABERS was easy and as an ex-teacher interested in poetry, so was METONYM.

Just to say: CATS are quite sociable. They like to be near one or on one and they cuddle up with each other. The mini clue will incite the ire of cat lovers. 

On the other hand, the midi was clever and cute.

Easy peasy.  60 across is a wee mistake,, because a kitten heel is  anti-stiletto. It is very short, with some thickness, and S- curved.  Tall girls such as myself loved them as teens because we would not tower over our boy dates.

"Not a problem!" from an internet company?


COMMENDABLE

1a [12 a.m., time to awake for Wordle]

COMEUPPANCE

So many themers!
That was cool.

And the DOT being the last to fall in the SE,
is *perfection* 
for a left-to-right,
top to bottom solver.
Like me!

I was finding it a *wee* stretchy to 
assume “the internet” 
could be simply shortened to COM,

but ( ploop! ) 
there it was waiting for me!


Happy hump day all!

1A [Quenched]

COMPENSATED

This was a clever theme with an even better execution.  I don't think it would be too difficult to come up with a long list of COM- words, and only a little more difficult to come up with COM- words in which the second half of the word was also a word in its own right,...
But it's extremely impressive to come up with so many COM- words where we don't notice the second half being a word, too, in which the meaning of the second word seems entirely unrelated to the meaning of the compound word.
Impressive!
COM+POST is my favorite example of this, with COM+PARABLE a close second.

Is this enough to make up for the "outrage" of SPARGE?  and its crossing with SOPOR?
You can decide that for yourself.

Excellent theme, Joseph Gangi!  Thanks for a fine Wednesday puzzle!

As a chemist (Ret.), I was familiar with SPARGE, although for a much different application than the one described in the clue. In my realm, sparging is a process in which a gas (usually an inert gas such as argon) is passed through a liquid to force the removal of other gases dissolved in the liquid.

Well, Joseph had me at SPARGE – it looks silly, is fun to say, and if you act like you know what you’re talking about, you can willy-nilly throw it into sentences.  
•	“Stay away from that guy – he’s recovering from a nasty case of sparge.”  
•	“… and that soupçon of sparge made the ravioli explode with flavor!”  

His theme was fun as well, as I tried to guess the theme answers from the clues.  Mostly struggled, got a couple, and my brain’s workout ethic was well satisfied.  Even more so from several areas of rub.

Then there was some lovely cluing originality – the first “waiter” pun clue in all Crosslandia for PATIENCE, as well as the first “seeds” pun clue for TOURNAMENT.  Plus, the original clue for ROAMING – [On a different network, say] – was a terrific misdirect for me.

Joseph, I love how your puzzles entertain as well as provide riddles to crack. I had a grand time with this one.  Thank you!
Looking back now it seems obvious, but during the solve I never even saw dot coming.

What a great puzzle with great fill. 

Not a college nickname to be seen. 

North East was a thing of beauty. 

ELOPER clue was perfect next to ROAMING. 

Off to use SPARGE in a conversation.....

1A [People together trying to change the law]

COMPETITION
Fun one, tougher than I was expecting for a Wednesday but maybe I'm just not awake yet

Although I enjoyed this puzzle quite a bit, I'm wondering now whether an opportunity was missed for a "true" rebus puzzle with a "." [DOT] as the rebus.

This seems so clever (and obvious?) now that I wonder whether it's already been done.

A couple days ago, I solved a Wednesday puzzle from the archives, from 6.Feb.2019. Not overly difficult, but of very elegant construction. I did it on my phone app, but I think the puzzle would benefit from being done on the print version. The puzzle was a debut by the memorably named Qweena Mewers, and I don't recall another by her, although xwordinfo tells me she's had one more published, about a year later, and none since. I guess Ms. Mewers is too busy working at the Orange County Superior Court, and curling. I also read wordplay, and the comments--193, which was quite a few for the time, including posts by persons who no longer, or rarely, post: Leapfinger, Andrew in Ottawa, and David Connell, for whom the puzzle provided ample fodder for etymological exegesis. https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2019/02/06

Whew. And Wow. Really slow start for me and was appropriately puzzled for a good long time. Finally working out the reveal and catching on to the trick was just a great 'aha' moment. Still a good workout filling in the rest but ended up being an enjoyable solve. And... appropriate puzzle find today. A Thursday from November 19, 2020 by Derek J. Angell. All of the theme answers referenced the answer at 55-across. Clue and answer for that: *"It debuted on 1/6/1975" WHLFRTN And some other theme answers (straightforwardly clued): *"Creator of 55-across" MRVGRFFN *"Co-host of 55-across" VNNWHT *"Co-host of 55-across" PTSJK *"Bad place to land on 55-across" BNKRPT And then... 13 down. "What you might cry when trying to answer the six starred clues? CANIBUYAVOWEL Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=11/19/2020&g=32&d=D ....
Anyone else have "mutton" at first for the shepard's pie ingredient?
Snickered as I worked this puzzle. Many fun clues.

A Cryptic-style puzzle with no suffering -- otherwise known as Cryptic-lite.  It was easy and breezy and I found it fun trying to guess the theme answers without crosses.

Of course, for those of us who do actual Cryptics, we know that in those, ALL the answers are some sort of trick answer or wordplay answer and not just the themers.  And that the large majority of tricks are a LOT harder than this one.

Is this a good puzzle for a Wed?  I've seen harder Wednesdays and I've seen easier ones, so I guess this is about right.  I found it very enjoyable.

SPARGE was an unknown to this solver, who enjoys beer but prefers it when someone else does the brewing. But etymologically predictable--it comes from Latin "spargere,"="to sprinkle," which traditionalist Catholics might recognize from the chant "Asperges me," and "aspergillium," that little stick that the priest uses to shower the congregation with Holy Water--Fr. Bede would always make sure to get me particularly wet. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Apda_-W1gt8&list=RDApda_-W1gt8&start_radio=1
Can anyone explain COM+POSER to me?
Lemme sparge my cabers!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ a message from the internet:

rinse your world with rose water
your strange pretty world
pick a windowpane
d/ little world people
the seeds of sleep say where…
for to be and to be me
this body this story
this might be the answer
a/ to see
Great Wednesday, but is JUST really okay as a clue for MERE? All the examples I can think of replace JUST with MERELY. (Just wondering...)

I loved this theme - perfect wordplay, IMO. Even with the COMmon starts (ha), each answer took me a couple crosses to figure out because of the changed pronunciation, which made it such a fun challenge. Thank you!
Tough NE corner. Sparge? And what isn't in shepherd's pie? Otherwise, a pretty breezy Wednesday, done before the coffee got cold. Good workout before the dreaded Thursday.
Clever!

Late to the party, having slept later after 9 nights on Pacific DST.  Kitty is on my lap, beginning to relax after DHubby "rescued" her from the vet's boarding unit.  
Now for the laundry, unpacking, restocking the larder, and then triage on the mail.

Clever puzzle theme (who thinks like this??) and fun solve...once I got over my first clue--the one about ____ Guofeng.  
Haven't we just seen NEMESIS?  

Some fresh vocab in this puzzle, which I welcome and celebrate!  CABERS, SOPOR, METONYM!  Not MERE bagatelles!
The quotation marks around "Wall Street" had me convinced it referred to the Michael Douglas movie. Excellent Wednesday theme; six themers, and not a clunker in the bunch.

Not a huge fan. Sloppy clueing IMO.

Rating:
6/10
Fun! Too many typos led to flyspecking, but that’s on me.

Liked "They usually get no reception" for ELOPERS.

Seemed about right for a Wednesday, and the theme was solid enough.
Super fun puzzle! The cluing sparkled and the theme was solid.  Thank you, Joseph Gangi!

A wanting theme today. Not a huge fan. The fact that all but one themed entry started COMP (and the other COMB) was a tad sloppy, and the revealers themselves felt deflated and uninteresting. The fill overall was nice and the cluing was pretty spot on, a good challenging puzzle for a Wednesday — even if some of the answers felt intentionally esoteric. A good puzzle, but one that could’ve used one more pass through editorial, I think.
There is a star, not a dot, preceding each of the major clues in the puzzle.

The puzzle was finished, all but the crossing of SPARGE and SOPOR.  I was unfamiliar with the first and was worried about the second.  I'm a big fan of torpor; that's what my little hummingbird friends do every night to rejuvenate their tiny, insatiable energetic bodies.  But it didn't fit.  So I typed in the S, preparing myself to be disappointed and boom -- I got the San Jose Strut and gold star.  I was shocked, I tell you.  Shocked.
i thought today's Midi was fun. now to actual Wednesday puzzle.

I hate it when I get too sure of myself and insist that my (wrong) answer is correct.  My example from today:  33D, clued as
"___ at 'em!".  I was sure that it was UPAND.

Yesterday's letters column highlighted a specific clue from the puzzle in titling itself "Boots on The Ground." 
Today's 3-Down answer is "Metonym."
--- FIGURES OF SPEECH POP QUIZ!!! ---
RIGHT NOTW put all your books and internets and AIs away, and get out a pencil and paper. There is only one question: 
1. Consider the phrase "Boots on the ground." Is this an example of:

A. Metonymy
B. Synechdochy
C. Both
D. Neither
Not nearly enough trivia 😂
That NE corner was brutal for me! I learned some new words today and thought this was a perfect Wednesday difficulty. Fun theme!

Unpacking (literally)--and I found the puzzle from last Sunday folded up at the bottom of the suitcase side pocket.  I had carried it with me as we flew first to DFW (a harrowing layover that included changing concourses using the rejiggered and badly overcrowded "SkyLink" trains undergoing construction) and then to Sea-Tac.  I see I never got the doubled letters entirely right (83A +64D, not 51D, silly goose!)  but I did complete the puzzle--and most of it correctly.  The Archive shows 877 Comments (now closed.)
The moral of this story:  Sunday puzzles and Travel may not be compatible!  I do remember (hazily) some pleasant Aha! feelings...  A merciful curtain of darkness now falls on this tragic scene.

Let's do more!

COMMODE: Standard company BS
COMMUTE: Stop spouting said company BS
COMPLAIN: Simplify
COMBINATION: At our company we consider all fellow citizens to eschew hetero- and homosexuality in favor of just a single label that covers both 

(Ok the last one may be a stretch but I couldn't resist)

It felt a bit tougher while doing it, but I finished up a good bit faster than my average—surprisingly. Although I got the theme while I was solving, I don't think I really appreciated it until afterward. Not sure why, but when I saw a themer at 1A, I didn't want to deal with it yet, so I switched to downs... and then, REBEL than I am, I started going willy-nilly all over the place, which was fun, but not my norm. 

Guess I just wanted to shake things up a bit on this fine day. But when one changes their ways so intensely, you have to wonder how it will impact the universe...   what will this kind of massive disturbance lead to?  A tear in the fabric of space and time, perhaps, changing the entire course of world history?! Human sacrifice, cats and dogs living together, mass hysteria!?!? Oh my! 

I have come to RUE my rash decision and apologize if my reckless actions caused any lasting damage to you fine solvers, if you're even still out there....  I promise to stay the course from now on

Nifty little puzzle for a Wednesday.

SPARGE sounds like a word Kramer would make up when playing Scrabble with Newman.

Thanks for the fun!

This had so many words I'd never heard of before but it wasn't tedious or frustrating at all, and the theme was so well constructed.  What a delightful puzzle! I also got 17 seconds on the mini today...PR is 16 and I never expected to get close to that again. 

I do usually think of kitten heels as being chunky so I was stunned when I searched them and saw so many narrow kitten heels...apparently they were developed as a more modest style of stiletto for younger teen girls. Learn something new every day.

Here's a POSER:

Is there a way to filter ngram viewer to limit hits of the noun POSER to the definition used in this puzzle, i.e. a (puzzling) question?

I'm lookin' at you, @Steve L.
A fine Wednesday puzzle. I got the missing . on the second one. A nice solve with just enough obscurity. Not a record but 7+ minutes faster than average.

Happy to see METONYM -- a favorite. Menotomy Park around the corner from my pad brings this word to mind. An excellent concept. Happy happy.
Didn't know kitten STILETTO a thing. Would have thought it an oxymoron.
What happened to "Abbrev." for ETC?
Sometimes I miss the tense. Oversell needed to be changed to OVERSOLD.
Never heard of SOPOR. Soporific, yes.
Uniform Resource LOCATOR. I'd forgotten 'bout that.
Wasn't sure how to spell JULEP.

Overall a fun puzzle for me -- right at the edge of my capabilities. I knew enough to take a shot, make mistakes, and figure it out. I'm finally learning to apply the patterns (noticing the COMs, the asterisks). It's like learning to think about the first or last halves of words in Connections. Yay puzzles.

"Paper view" was pretty nice. "Recipient of many dad jokes" should have included ", perhaps". As always, common phrases do not belong in a NYT puzzle, so shame on you there. Good puzzle overall, though, imho. Thanks.
I was certain “Pirates Booty” was ARSE [ARRRSE].

Internet company's dorm room wall decor? COMPOSTER. Internet company's way of doing things? COMMODE. Internet company's fraternal cannibalistic retort? COMEATMEBRO. Thanks, you've been a great audience.

Somehow got the Wednesday, but not Monday and Tuesday this week lol. Good puzzle though

Sopor should probably have a spanish indicator next to it, even if it is technically a word

Fun puzzle.

Got a pleasant aha when I figured out the theme.

Kind of a Wednesday and a half, but I'm not complaining.
Perhaps I'm overthinking the revealer...  is DOT COM PARABLE supposed to be the phrase? Or is it just DOT COM?
Fun!
I found this one pretty comfortable, but still fun! Just finished my last ever (!!) college essay (yay commencement!) so not complaining about a comely theme. compliments to the creator :)
Selective Walrus — Nobody I've met uses poser for a question, but this is U.S. slang I've seen many times — usually meaning a riddle or brain-teaser, not just any old question.

@Selective Walrus 
Leave it to crosswords to keep archaic word forms alive :)

@John 
Hey, I take exception to that! I've heard it many times, and I'm not archaic. Yet....

@Selective Walrus
In that context, I was reminded of APER, referring to a copier. Cheap, opportunistic and a major stretch, but very effective.

@Selective Walrus It’s an old-fashioned use of the word that has fallen far from popular usage. So, while still technically a fair use of the word here, I agree it feels out of place. Others will cling to their dictionaries and swear that any word from any time is fair game, but I don’t agree.

@NanuNanu
I myself take umbrage. ;)
srsly, I've heard and used it often. as in
"here's a poser; what percentage of solvers were familiar with the term 'SPARGE'?"

@ardelion

The Midi is shy.  It has to be coaxed out.

Also hard to see.  That's why it's often Not Found.

The Midi is the J. D. Salinger of the puzzle world.

@SBK in TO
good catch on the correction, and great job associating Laura with the midi. I remember a 1987 film version directed by Paul Newman; the cast was:
Amanda Wingfield: Joanne Woodward
Tom Wingfield: John Malkovich
Laura Wingfield: Karen Allen
The Gentleman Caller: James Naughton
@Steve L Yes, in fact I had restaurant waiters in mind when I was doing the puzzle. It was only when I read today's blog that I realized the answer could refer to anyone who waits. Duh.
I think that if the clue were referring to waitstaff, it wouldn't end with a question mark.

@Bill in Yokohama 
Although at times it is an absolutely essential trait for waitstaff.

I just mean that the question mark indicates word play, i.e. the clue should not be read literally.

Of course, as Steve L pointed out at the start, it works for waitstaff too.
@Chris definitely not making that up

@Chris I had the exact same concern Monday (though my repeat word was OHARE).  I did not realize AI was such a culprit until one responder explained it.  In a vacuum I’m sure it’s fine, but for some regulars it can be jarring.

Reminds me of a recent HBR article on AI Workslop.  Not quite the same situation but it does illustrate a drawback.  Makes me wonder if this could be a reason why the level of difficulty has decreased in past years.

@Chris

I owe DEL MAR being a gimme to "Weeds" :D

@Andrzej
I entered REN MAR until it hit me. See how too much Weed can affect your thinking?

@Jerry

I should explain - wondering if Ren Mar was real, I did some research and learned of Del Mar. So the "Weeds" connection is indirect, I guess.
@Chris "Sopor" was also, fittingly, a brand name for quaaludes. In recreational context, it was pronounced "soaper". Or so a friend told me.

@Andrzej

Fans of The Sport of Kings will know of the thoroughbred race track in Del Mar.  "Where the turf meets the surf."

@Andrzej
I would have clicked on Recommend, but there's no way I'm going to recommend a photo with that scowling, ugly, Fascist face on it. I guess there's nothing sacred left in the country anymore. So sad that my traveling days are over, but glad that I won't be renewing my passport.

@Andrzej
I wonder if one will get a choice..and if not, if they'll still be there when he's no longer in office. Luckily my passport won't be up for renewal for a while.

@Andrzej

I don't know what I'm going to do with all the vomit.

@john ezra I saw a lot of cabers being tossed when I was attending the annual Highland Games at Ligonier many years ago. My Westinghouse colleague Paul Thompson and his wife Sara Jean always filled major roles in the proceedings. A quick search shows that the Games continue although the date for 2026 has been moved from the fall to July. The link includes the shocking sad news that Sara Jean passed away recently. https://www.ligonierhighlandgames.org/flowers-of-the-forest.html

@john ezra

I'm much smaller than a caber but I weigh more. And I'd be especially tough to get a grip on for the throw if you polished me.
@Andrzej -- now that Al has told me that the local highland games have been rescheduled for July, I better start training early. Luckily I have a buddy here in town from Krakow to practice on!

@Andrzej
You're already polished.

@john ezra
I'm told that after the competition, "adult entertainers" will be putting on a Pole dancing demonstration.

@john ezra
i love everything about this thread. I will only add that your post sent me back to ngram viewer to compare SPARGE and CABER. They cross a surprising number of times, but in the last ten years SPARGE  took quite a dive and ended up below CABER after dominance since the mid-1930s.
@John oh, NE
@Spmm Escalators are the moving staircases.
...and in the U.S., lifts are called elevators.

@Matt

Keep your caber in your pants.

@Matt
indeed. in fact, perhaps even Callooh! Callay!

@Andrzej
I've heard that Skywalker had a light caber.

Heidi,
Closely following duplicate answers were a "feature" of Shortz era crosswords, noted here and elsewhere, long before AI.

@Barry Ancona

@Bill Latinists and music lovers will also recognize "sparge" from the verse of the "Dies Irae" (from the Requiem Mass) "Tuba mirum spargens sonum," which is often set as a separate movement. Here, "spargens" is used in the sense of "scattering," or "spreading," but I can't help but to imagine the Last Trumpet *sprinkling* its wondrous sound. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6u1IkXesHMQ (Mr. Brevik, tell your buddy to buy a camera tripod--I'm getting dizzy!)

@Mean Old Lady:
LOL. You have my empathy on your DFW train wreck which, unfortunately, I can top (bottom?). Right before we landed last November, a severe thunderstorm had just moved away from Dallas, blowing up the DFW schedule for the rest of the day, with every flight canceled or delayed. We'd get a text saying we'd been auto-rescheduled with maybe 35 minutes to departure, having to change terminals via SkyLink, and of course missing the rescheduled flight, rinse and repeat, ALWAYS having to change terminals and walk long distances from the train. Honestly it happened four or five times before we finally made a flight. Our bags got to the destination before we got there, exhausted, seven hours late. We'd have been better off stuffing ourselves into our checked suitcases. My wife declared DFW: Never Again!

@Mean Old Lady and Clutch Cargo

Ohhh, DFW is one of the airports I try very hard to avoid! That, MIA and the one out of Newark are my three worst airport experiences. Far worse than flying into either Siberia or Honduras.

On the bright side, my performance on the flight out of Newark will probably never be forgotten by many of the people on board. I don't want to brag, but the children probably needed counseling afterward. 

After several delays and a super hot airport (AC was out), and having to run between gates because they had made us change flights, I was already a bit off.  And now the new plane was a teeny tiny tin can (1x2 seater). I have serious flight anxiety; I've learned to manage it drug-free on big planes (3x3 seaters), but this was so tiny, the second I walked on, I started a full-on panic attack. I stopped and couldn't go further, people were backing up behind me, getting peeved...  my husband trying to push me forward, me dropping f bom — bs (not my norm!). The flight attendant asking if I need to leave. Me wanting desperately to be home, I started walking down the aisle "f word, f word, f word," mothers looking at me in horror... I get to my seat and yell Xanax!!!!  Fortunately for all, I always carry some on a flight, just in case, but hadn't needed one in years. 

Turns out, I also had pink eye, but didn't know it yet. 😬 It developed more along the flight back to the wonderful MSP airport, where we proceeded directly to urgent care.

@Mean Old Lady
When I was stationed at Goodfellow AFB (Army has an MI school there) I had to go through DFW a couple of times. I left a small case of cassettes on the seat of the SkyLink, and had only one tape to listen to on my Walkman for the rest of the flight. I can't blame the airport for that, though.

@Clutch Cargo
Wow!  I counter with the fact that my husband and I were traveling because we were going to celebrate his 85th birthday!  We had not asked for any assistance even though he needs a cane because supposedly we weren't changing terminals.  Every time we turned a corner, there was another 100 yard hallway.  None of the moving walkways were working.  We were pulling carry-ons and lugging his c-pap in addition to our one "personal item such as a purse."  (I'm not young, either...)  But yeah.....You still win...

@Grant
LOL  yeah, you don't even move the needle, but I still feel for you.  Losing your music could constitute an emergency!

@Clutch Cargo

The worst experience I ever had on a plane was trying to fly into DFW from Las Vegas on my way back to Tennessee.  About 30 years ago, I guess.

We got caught in what felt to me like a monster thunderstorm, and we were being whipped all over the place.  It was the birth of my era a fearful flying (and one occasion of turning back at the departure gate because I couldn't go through with it).

I spent a good deal of that flight counting backwards from 1000 by 7s, something I've done many times since.

It all ending in late 2016.  For some wild reason, I suddenly realized that life was not necessarily better than the alternative.
